text: Do you use the same password for multiple online accounts?

options.text: Yes, I use the same 1 or 2 passwords for almost everything.
Sometimes, but only for accounts that aren't important.
Never. Every single account has a unique password.

recommendation.title: Start Using a Password Manager
recommendation.description: Reusing passwords is the #1 way accounts get hacked. A password manager will remember unique passwords for you.

text: How do you store and remember your passwords?

options.text: I write them down on paper or in a plain text file/notes app.
I let my web browser save them.
I use a dedicated, encrypted Password Manager (e.g., Bitwarden, 1Password).

recommendation.title: Secure Your Written Passwords
recommendation.description: Plain text files and notebooks are easily compromised. Migrate them to an encrypted app like Bitwarden or 1Password.

text: When creating a new password, what is your standard format?

options.text: A memorable word with a number at the end (e.g., Summer2024!).
A random mix of characters, but shorter than 12 characters.
A passphrase or random string that is 14+ characters long.

recommendation.title: Switch to Passphrases
recommendation.description: Length beats complexity. Instead of short passwords with symbols, use 4 random words strung together (e.g., horse-battery-staple-correct).

text: Do you have Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) enabled on your critical accounts (Email, Banking)?

options.text: No, it takes too much time to log in.
Yes, I receive an SMS text message with a code.
Yes, I use an Authenticator App (e.g., Authy, Google Auth) or a hardware key.

recommendation.title: Enable 2FA on Critical Accounts
recommendation.description: Turn on Two-Factor Authentication (preferably via an Authenticator App) for your primary Email and Bank accounts immediately.

text: If a company you have an account with announces a data breach, what do you do?

options.text: Nothing, it usually doesn't affect me.
I change my password for that specific site eventually.
I immediately change that password and any other accounts that shared it.

recommendation.title: React Faster to Data Breaches
recommendation.description: Register your email on HaveIBeenPwned.com to get automated alerts if your data leaks, so you can change passwords instantly.

text: How often do you change passwords for your most important accounts (email, banking, primary social media)?

options.text: Rarely or never, unless I'm locked out.
Only when a site forces me to change it.
Proactively every 3–12 months or after any suspicious activity.

recommendation.title: Adopt Regular Password Rotation
recommendation.description: Schedule periodic password updates for critical accounts so that a stolen password becomes useless faster.

text: How do you handle default passwords on new devices or accounts?

options.text: I leave default passwords as they are.
I change them only if prompted or when I remember.
I always change default passwords immediately during setup.

recommendation.title: Eliminate Default Passwords
recommendation.description: Default passwords are widely known to attackers. Always replace them with strong, unique credentials right after setup.

text: Do you use a password manager across all your devices (laptop, phone, tablet)?

options.text: No, I don't use a password manager at all.
Yes, but only on one device or inconsistently.
Yes, it's installed and synced on every device I use.

recommendation.title: Enable Cross-Device Sync for Your Password Manager
recommendation.description: Using your password manager everywhere avoids the temptation to reuse or simplify passwords on secondary devices.

text: Have you ever shared a password with someone else (family, friends, colleagues)?

options.text: Yes, I frequently share passwords when needed.
Only for less important accounts like streaming services.
No, I never share passwords. I use features like 'family sharing' instead.

recommendation.title: Stop Sharing Passwords Directly
recommendation.description: Shared passwords quickly lose control. Use official sharing features or separate accounts so you can revoke access safely.

text: How strong are the passwords for your email accounts (the accounts used for password resets)?

options.text: They are simple and easy to remember.
They are moderately complex but reused elsewhere.
They are long, unique, and stored in a password manager.

recommendation.title: Harden Your Email Passwords
recommendation.description: Email accounts control password resets. Treat them like master keys and secure them with strong, unique credentials.

text: Do your passwords include easily guessable information (birthdays, names, favorite teams)?

options.text: Yes, I usually include personal info so I don't forget.
Sometimes, but I mix it with other characters.
Never. I avoid any personal information in passwords.

recommendation.title: Remove Personal Clues From Passwords
recommendation.description: Attackers use your public information to guess passwords. Rely on random words or manager-generated strings instead.

text: When a site offers 'Sign in with Google/Apple/Facebook', how do you decide?

options.text: I use social login everywhere without thinking about it.
I sometimes use it for convenience on low-risk sites.
I deliberately choose trusted providers with strong security and 2FA, and avoid over-linking accounts.

recommendation.title: Use Single Sign-On Strategically
recommendation.description: Central logins can be secure if protected with strong passwords and 2FA, but avoid linking everything to one account.

text: How do you handle password resets when you forget a password?

options.text: I just reuse an old or similar password each time.
I create a new password, but often reuse patterns.
I generate a brand-new unique password and store it securely.

recommendation.title: Break Predictable Password Patterns
recommendation.description: Repeated patterns like 'Password2024', 'Password2025' are easy to guess. Let your manager generate new passwords each time.

text: Have you ever checked if your passwords or emails appear in known data breaches?

options.text: No, I have never checked.
I checked once or twice in the past.
I regularly check and monitor for breaches using services like HaveIBeenPwned.

recommendation.title: Monitor for Credential Leaks
recommendation.description: Use reputable breach-checking tools and set alerts so you can update compromised passwords immediately.

text: How do you handle passwords on shared or public computers (e.g., cybercafé, friend's laptop)?

options.text: I log in normally and let the browser save passwords if convenient.
I avoid saving passwords but still log in directly.
I avoid logging into sensitive accounts or use incognito + password manager + manual logout.

recommendation.title: Avoid Saving Passwords on Shared Devices
recommendation.description: Never let shared machines remember your passwords, and log out fully after use to prevent hijacking.

text: Do you use simple PINs or patterns for device unlock instead of strong passwords?

options.text: Yes, I use an easy 4‑digit PIN or simple pattern.
I use a moderately complex PIN or pattern.
I use strong PINs, passwords, or biometrics configured securely.

recommendation.title: Strengthen Device Unlock Methods
recommendation.description: Simple PINs can be shoulder‑surfed. Use longer PINs, complex passwords, and biometric features where available.

text: How do you manage passwords for work and personal accounts?

options.text: I often reuse the same or similar passwords between work and personal accounts.
I try to separate them, but there is some overlap.
Work and personal accounts always have distinct, unique passwords.

recommendation.title: Separate Work and Personal Credentials
recommendation.description: Keep professional and personal passwords completely distinct so one breach does not compromise everything.

text: How often do you install operating system updates on your primary devices (phone, laptop)?

options.text: I ignore updates or postpone them indefinitely.
I install updates occasionally when I have time.
I install updates promptly or have automatic updates enabled.

recommendation.title: Enable Automatic Updates
recommendation.description: Security patches fix known vulnerabilities. Turn on automatic updates for your OS and key apps.

text: Do you use reputable antivirus or endpoint protection software on your devices?

options.text: No, I don't use any antivirus or protection tools.
I use free or built‑in protection but rarely check it.
I use well‑known security software and keep it updated with regular scans.

recommendation.title: Install and Maintain Security Software
recommendation.description: Reliable security tools help detect malware, ransomware, and other threats before they cause damage.

text: How quickly do you install security patches for browsers, messaging apps, and critical software?

options.text: I rarely update apps unless they break.
I update apps sometimes but not urgently.
I update apps as soon as updates are available or use auto‑update.

recommendation.title: Prioritize Application Updates
recommendation.description: Browsers and communication apps are common attack targets. Keep them fully patched at all times.

text: Do you use a lock screen (PIN, password, biometric) on your smartphone and laptop?

options.text: No, my devices unlock without any security.
I use a simple PIN or pattern that is easy to guess.
I use strong PINs/passwords and/or biometrics on all devices.

recommendation.title: Lock All Devices
recommendation.description: Unprotected devices expose email, banking, and messages. Always require a PIN, password, or biometric to unlock.

text: Is full-disk encryption enabled on your laptop and mobile devices (e.g., BitLocker, FileVault, Android/iOS encryption)?

options.text: No, my devices are not encrypted or I am not sure.
Some devices are encrypted, others are not.
All devices that support it have full‑disk encryption enabled.

recommendation.title: Turn On Device Encryption
recommendation.description: If your device is lost or stolen, encryption keeps your data unreadable without your password.

text: How do you handle device backups?

options.text: I rarely or never back up my devices.
I back up manually from time to time.
I have automatic, encrypted backups configured (local or cloud).

recommendation.title: Configure Automatic Backups
recommendation.description: Regular backups protect you against ransomware, hardware failure, and accidental deletion.

text: Do you install apps or software from unofficial or pirated sources?

options.text: Yes, I often use cracked or unofficial versions.
Occasionally, if I trust the source.
Never. I only install from official stores or verified vendors.

recommendation.title: Avoid Pirated and Untrusted Software
recommendation.description: Unofficial downloads are a major malware risk. Stick to official stores and trusted vendors only.

text: How do you handle USB drives and external media from unknown sources?

options.text: I plug them in and open files without concern.
I scan them sometimes with antivirus.
I avoid unknown media or always scan them before opening any file.

recommendation.title: Treat Unknown USB Devices as Risky
recommendation.description: Malicious USBs can infect devices quickly. Only use trusted drives and scan them first.

text: Do you allow your devices to auto-connect to any available Bluetooth or nearby devices?

options.text: Yes, I leave Bluetooth on and connect freely.
I sometimes disable it in public places.
I keep Bluetooth off when not needed and control pairing requests.

recommendation.title: Limit Unnecessary Wireless Connections
recommendation.description: Disable Bluetooth and other radios when not needed to reduce attack surfaces.

text: Have you enabled 'Find My Device' or equivalent tracking/wipe features?

options.text: No, I have not enabled any tracking or remote wipe features.
I enabled it on some devices but not all.
All my devices have tracking and remote wipe configured.

recommendation.title: Set Up Remote Locate and Wipe
recommendation.description: If a device is lost, remote tracking and wiping help protect your data and accounts.

text: Do you use separate user accounts on shared computers at home or work?

options.text: No, everyone uses the same account.
There are some separate accounts but shared in practice.
Each person has their own password‑protected account.

recommendation.title: Use Individual User Accounts
recommendation.description: Separate accounts reduce accidental changes and limit the spread of malware or misconfigurations.

text: Are you still using devices or operating systems that no longer receive security updates?

options.text: Yes, I regularly use unsupported devices/OS versions.
A few older devices are still in use.
I avoid unsupported systems and upgrade before end‑of‑support.

recommendation.title: Retire Unsupported Systems
recommendation.description: Old systems without patches are easy targets. Upgrade or isolate them from sensitive activities.

text: Do you jailbreak or root your smartphone?

options.text: Yes, my primary phone is rooted/jailbroken.
I experimented on a secondary device only.
No, I keep my devices in the default, supported configuration.

recommendation.title: Avoid Rooting/Jailbreaking Daily-Use Devices
recommendation.description: Rooted devices often bypass security controls and are more vulnerable to malware.

text: How do you secure physical access to your devices when traveling or in public spaces?

options.text: I often leave devices unattended or unlocked.
I lock them sometimes but not consistently.
I lock devices, keep them in sight, and avoid leaving them unattended.

recommendation.title: Protect Devices Physically
recommendation.description: Physical theft can bypass digital controls. Keep devices near you and locked when unattended.

text: Do you review your device security settings (firewall, privacy, permissions) periodically?

options.text: No, I never look at these settings.
Occasionally, when I notice something unusual.
Yes, I periodically review and tighten my security settings.

recommendation.title: Regularly Audit Device Settings
recommendation.description: Periodic reviews help you catch misconfigurations and unnecessary exposures early.

text: How do you handle notifications that appear on your lock screen (messages, OTPs, emails)?

options.text: Everything is visible on the lock screen, including full messages.
Some sensitive content is hidden, but not all.
I hide sensitive content or turn off lock‑screen previews for private apps.

recommendation.title: Limit Sensitive Lock-Screen Content
recommendation.description: Lock‑screen previews can reveal OTPs and private data. Restrict what is shown when the device is locked.

text: Do you use a personal firewall or router firewall to protect your home devices?

options.text: No, I rely only on my ISP's default setup.
I use a basic router firewall with default settings.
I ensure firewall features are enabled and properly configured.

recommendation.title: Verify Firewall Protection
recommendation.description: A correctly configured firewall adds a strong layer of defence against unsolicited inbound traffic.

text: What is the default visibility of your main social media profiles?

options.text: Public. Anyone can see my posts and profile details.
Partially restricted with some privacy settings enabled.
Mostly private, limited to friends/contacts I approve.

recommendation.title: Tighten Profile Privacy
recommendation.description: Set your profiles to friends‑only and review what non‑contacts can see about you.

text: How often do you review and adjust social media privacy settings?

options.text: Never. I leave them at default.
Occasionally, when prompted by the platform.
Regularly, at least a few times per year.

recommendation.title: Schedule Privacy Checkups
recommendation.description: Platforms frequently change features. Regular reviews keep your exposure under control.

text: What kind of personal information do you share publicly on social media?

options.text: I post details like address, phone number, or travel plans.
I share some personal info but avoid the most sensitive details.
I avoid posting sensitive personal or location information publicly.

recommendation.title: Reduce Sensitive Public Posts
recommendation.description: Oversharing helps attackers guess passwords and target scams. Keep critical details offline.

text: How do you handle friend/follow requests from people you do not recognize?

options.text: I often accept them to grow my network.
I sometimes accept if we have mutual connections.
I only accept requests from people I know or can verify.

recommendation.title: Screen Connection Requests Carefully
recommendation.description: Fake profiles can harvest data or send malicious links. Only connect with verified people.

text: Do you click on links or open attachments received via social media messages from unknown or semi-known contacts?

options.text: Yes, I often click if the content looks interesting.
I sometimes click but try to be cautious.
I rarely click unfamiliar links and verify with the sender first.

recommendation.title: Treat Social Media Links as Suspicious
recommendation.description: Phishing and malware often arrive via DMs. Verify unexpected links before opening.

text: Do you use Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on your social media accounts?

options.text: No, it's not enabled on any account.
Enabled on some important accounts.
Enabled on all main social accounts, using an authenticator app when possible.

recommendation.title: Enable 2FA on Social Platforms
recommendation.description: Account takeovers are very common. 2FA stops many attacks even if your password leaks.

text: How often do you review and revoke third-party apps connected to your social media accounts?

options.text: Never. I forget which apps have access.
Occasionally, when notified of suspicious activity.
Regularly review and remove apps I no longer use.

recommendation.title: Audit Connected Apps
recommendation.description: Old games and apps can still access your data. Revoke permissions for anything unnecessary.

text: Do you allow social media platforms to automatically tag your location in posts?

options.text: Yes, location is usually enabled.
Sometimes, mainly for special events.
I disable location tagging or use it very selectively.

recommendation.title: Limit Location Sharing
recommendation.description: Real‑time location data can enable stalking or burglary. Share locations sparingly and after leaving.

text: How do you respond to online quizzes and surveys that ask for personal details (e.g., first pet's name)?

options.text: I often participate and give real answers.
Sometimes I answer but avoid the most sensitive questions.
I avoid such quizzes or give fake, non-identifying answers.

recommendation.title: Avoid Sharing Security Question Clues
recommendation.description: Quizzes often harvest answers used for password resets. Never share real security-question data.

text: Do you use unique passwords for your social media accounts compared to other sites?

options.text: No, I reuse passwords across social and other sites.
Some accounts share passwords, others are unique.
All social accounts have distinct, strong passwords.

recommendation.title: Isolate Social Media Passwords
recommendation.description: If a forum is breached, reused passwords can expose your social accounts. Make them unique.

text: How do you manage who can tag you or post on your timeline?

options.text: Anyone can tag me or post on my timeline.
Friends can tag/post, but I rarely review tags.
I review tags/posts before they appear or have strict controls.

recommendation.title: Control Tagging and Timeline Posts
recommendation.description: Restrict who can tag or post to reduce impersonation and reputation risks.

text: When you stop using a social media platform, what do you do with the account?

options.text: I simply stop logging in and forget about it.
I sometimes change the password first.
I deactivate or delete old accounts whenever possible.

recommendation.title: Close Unused Social Accounts
recommendation.description: Dormant accounts can be hijacked. Delete or deactivate platforms you no longer use.

text: Do you separate professional and personal identities across social platforms?

options.text: No, I use the same profiles for everything.
Partially separated but still mixed content.
Yes, I maintain clearly separated professional and personal profiles.

recommendation.title: Differentiate Professional and Personal Profiles
recommendation.description: Separation helps you manage privacy and reduce the impact of any single compromise.

text: How do you respond if friends or followers report receiving strange messages from your account?

options.text: I ignore it or assume it's a glitch.
I change my password later and delete odd posts.
I immediately change password, check logins, revoke apps, and alert contacts.

recommendation.title: Act Quickly on Account Takeover Signs
recommendation.description: Fast action limits damage if your account is hijacked. Secure it and inform your network.

text: Do you enable login alerts or new device notifications on social media platforms?

options.text: No, I don't use these alerts.
Enabled on some accounts only.
Enabled on all major platforms I use.

recommendation.title: Turn On Login Alerts
recommendation.description: Login alerts give you an early warning if someone else signs into your account.

text: How carefully do you consider the audience (friends lists, groups) before posting sensitive updates?

options.text: I usually post without adjusting audience settings.
Sometimes I choose a smaller audience for personal posts.
I actively choose audiences and use lists/groups for sensitive topics.

recommendation.title: Use Audience Controls
recommendation.description: Not every update needs to be public. Segment audiences to reduce unnecessary exposure.

text: Do you educate family members (especially children or elders) about safe social media habits?

options.text: No, I have not discussed social media safety with them.
We have had a few informal conversations.
I regularly guide them and help configure their privacy and security settings.

recommendation.title: Extend Safety Practices to Family
recommendation.description: Attackers can target your relatives to reach you. Share good habits with them as well.

text: How do you react to emails from unknown senders with links or attachments?

options.text: I often open them if the subject looks interesting or urgent.
I am cautious but sometimes click if it seems legitimate.
I verify the sender and link independently before interacting, or delete them.

recommendation.title: Treat Unsolicited Emails as Suspicious
recommendation.description: Phishing emails often appear urgent. Verify through official channels before clicking.

text: Do you check email sender addresses carefully (not just display names)?

options.text: No, I mostly look at the display name and subject.
Sometimes I check the full address if something seems off.
I regularly inspect the full sender address and domain.

recommendation.title: Inspect Sender Domains
recommendation.description: Attackers spoof names but not domains perfectly. Check full addresses for subtle differences.

text: How do you handle emails that ask for passwords, OTPs, or financial information?

options.text: I may reply if I think the request is from a trusted organization.
I sometimes respond after quickly checking the context.
I never share such details by email and contact the organization via official channels instead.

recommendation.title: Never Send Sensitive Data via Email
recommendation.description: Legitimate organizations will not ask for passwords or OTPs over email. Use official websites or apps instead.

text: Do you hover over links in emails to preview the actual URL before clicking?

options.text: No, I usually just click links directly.
Sometimes, if the email seems suspicious.
Yes, I routinely preview links and type important URLs manually.

recommendation.title: Preview Links Before Clicking
recommendation.description: Hovering over links reveals the real destination and helps you avoid fake login pages.

text: How do you unsubscribe from unwanted marketing emails?

options.text: I click any 'unsubscribe' link I see, even in suspicious emails.
I sometimes unsubscribe, sometimes mark as spam.
I only unsubscribe from clearly legitimate senders and mark others as spam.

recommendation.title: Use Spam Reporting Safely
recommendation.description: Unsubscribing from malicious emails confirms your address. Mark them as spam instead.

text: Do you use spam filters and phishing protection features in your email service?

options.text: No, or I have disabled them.
I use default settings but rarely review spam folders.
I keep filters enabled and review spam folders carefully for false positives.

recommendation.title: Leverage Built-In Email Protection
recommendation.description: Modern email services provide strong filters. Keep them enabled and tuned.

text: How do you respond to emails claiming to be from your bank or payment provider with urgent actions?

options.text: I click the link or attachment if it looks genuine.
I might call a number in the email to confirm.
I log in via the official website/app or call the number printed on my card.

recommendation.title: Verify Financial Emails Out-of-Band
recommendation.description: Never trust contact info in the message. Use known official channels instead.

text: Do you separate email addresses for different purposes (banking, shopping, newsletters)?

options.text: No, I use the same email everywhere.
I have a couple of emails but reuse them widely.
I use separate or alias emails for critical services vs general use.

recommendation.title: Segment Email Identities
recommendation.description: Different addresses reduce spam on your main account and limit damage if one address is leaked.

text: How do you handle unexpected attachments, even from known contacts?

options.text: I open them if they come from someone I know.
I sometimes verify if the message seems odd.
I confirm with the sender via another channel before opening suspicious attachments.

recommendation.title: Verify Suspicious Attachments
recommendation.description: Compromised accounts can send malware to their contacts. Confirm unexpected files before opening.

text: Are you familiar with common phishing indicators (typos, mismatched URLs, generic greetings)?

options.text: Not really; I haven't learned about them.
I know some signs but don't always check.
Yes, I actively look for these red flags in every email.

recommendation.title: Learn Phishing Red Flags
recommendation.description: Train yourself to spot suspicious patterns so you can avoid falling for scams.

text: Do you use email encryption or secure messaging for highly sensitive communications?

options.text: No, I send everything in plain email.
Occasionally I use secure channels when reminded.
I use end‑to‑end encrypted tools or email encryption for sensitive topics.

recommendation.title: Use Encrypted Channels for Sensitive Data
recommendation.description: Plain email is like a postcard. Use end‑to‑end encrypted apps or email encryption when privacy matters.

text: How do you respond to job offers, lottery wins, or prize notifications received by email?

options.text: I often reply or click to see what I won.
I sometimes verify but still explore some offers.
I treat them as scams unless I can independently confirm legitimacy.

recommendation.title: Assume Unexpected Windfalls Are Scams
recommendation.description: Most unsolicited prizes and offers are fraud. Delete or verify via official websites.

text: Do you regularly review security and login activity logs for your email accounts?

options.text: No, I never check these logs.
Occasionally, if I suspect something.
Yes, I periodically review recent logins and devices.

recommendation.title: Monitor Email Login Activity
recommendation.description: Reviewing login history helps you spot unauthorized access early.

text: Do you use Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) on your primary email accounts?

options.text: No, only passwords.
Enabled on at least one primary email.
Enabled on all primary and recovery emails.

recommendation.title: Protect Email With 2FA
recommendation.description: Email is the key to many other accounts. Secure it with 2FA to prevent takeovers.

text: Do you have separate email addresses for account recovery versus daily communication?

options.text: No, I use one email for everything.
I have multiple emails but they all mix uses.
I keep a dedicated, well‑protected email for recovery and critical services.

recommendation.title: Use a Hardened Recovery Email
recommendation.description: A dedicated recovery email, secured with 2FA, reduces the chance of broad account compromise.

text: Do you train yourself or your team using phishing simulations or awareness materials?

options.text: No, I haven't done any structured training.
I have read some articles or taken a basic course.
I regularly participate in phishing simulations or formal training.

recommendation.title: Invest Time in Phishing Awareness
recommendation.description: Practice recognizing scams through training or simulations to build strong instincts.

text: How do you handle email access on public or shared computers?

options.text: I sign in normally and sometimes forget to log out.
I log out but let the browser remember my email.
I use private mode, avoid saving credentials, and log out completely.

recommendation.title: Secure Email on Shared Devices
recommendation.description: Never save passwords on shared machines and always log out fully when finished.

text: How strong is your home Wi-Fi password?

options.text: It's short, simple, or still the default.
It's moderately complex but reused elsewhere.
It's long, unique, and only shared with trusted people.

recommendation.title: Harden Your Wi-Fi Password
recommendation.description: Your home network password should be unique and hard to guess to keep intruders out.

text: Have you changed the default admin username and password on your home router?

options.text: No, they are still the factory defaults.
I changed one of them (username or password).
Yes, both admin username and password are unique and strong.

recommendation.title: Change Router Admin Credentials
recommendation.description: Default router logins are widely known. Update them immediately to block easy access.

text: What type of Wi-Fi encryption is your home network using?

options.text: Open or WEP/no idea which one.
WPA or WPA2 without checking details.
WPA2 or WPA3 with a strong password configured.

recommendation.title: Use WPA2 or WPA3 Encryption
recommendation.description: Old standards like WEP are broken. Ensure your router uses WPA2 or WPA3.

text: Do you have a separate guest Wi-Fi network for visitors?

options.text: No, guests use the same network as my devices.
Sometimes I change my main Wi-Fi password for guests.
Yes, guests use a separate, isolated Wi-Fi network.

recommendation.title: Enable a Guest Network
recommendation.description: Guest networks limit what visitors can reach and keep your devices more isolated.

text: How do you use public Wi-Fi (cafés, airports, malls)?

options.text: I connect to any free Wi-Fi and do everything, including banking.
I avoid sensitive activities but still connect freely.
I limit use to low-risk browsing and prefer VPNs or mobile data for sensitive tasks.

recommendation.title: Be Careful on Public Wi-Fi
recommendation.description: Treat public Wi‑Fi as untrusted. Use VPN or mobile data for logins and financial activity.

text: Do you allow your devices to auto-connect to any remembered Wi-Fi networks?

options.text: Yes, my device automatically joins any known network.
I sometimes forget to disable auto-connect.
I disable auto-connect for public networks and prune old entries.

recommendation.title: Disable Auto-Connect on Public Networks
recommendation.description: Attackers can mimic known Wi‑Fi names. Control which networks your device joins automatically.

text: How often do you update your router's firmware?

options.text: Never or I did it only once when installing.
Occasionally, when I notice a problem.
I periodically check for and apply firmware updates.

recommendation.title: Keep Router Firmware Updated
recommendation.description: Firmware updates patch security vulnerabilities in your network equipment.

text: Do you use a VPN on unsecured or public networks?

options.text: No, I never use a VPN.
Sometimes, especially when working remotely.
Yes, I routinely enable a reputable VPN on untrusted networks.

recommendation.title: Use a VPN on Untrusted Networks
recommendation.description: VPNs encrypt your traffic, making it harder for others to snoop on public Wi‑Fi.

text: How do you manage IoT devices (smart bulbs, cameras, speakers) on your network?

options.text: All devices share the same network without special configuration.
I change some defaults but keep them on the main network.
I change default passwords and, where possible, place IoT on a separate VLAN/guest network.

recommendation.title: Segment IoT Devices
recommendation.description: Smart devices are often less secure. Isolating them limits impact if they are compromised.

text: Do you ever connect to open Wi-Fi networks that do not require a password?

options.text: Yes, frequently and for most activities.
Occasionally, but I avoid logins if I can.
Rarely, and only for low‑risk browsing with a VPN if necessary.

recommendation.title: Avoid Open Wi-Fi When Possible
recommendation.description: Open networks are easy to monitor. Prefer secured networks or mobile data.

text: Have you disabled remote administration on your home router (unless strictly needed)?

options.text: No, remote admin is enabled or I am not sure.
I think it is disabled, but I have not checked.
Yes, remote management is off or securely restricted to VPN.

recommendation.title: Disable Unnecessary Remote Access
recommendation.description: Remote admin greatly increases exposure. Turn it off unless you have a strong reason and secure setup.

text: Do you regularly review which devices are connected to your home network?

options.text: No, I never check.
Occasionally, when something seems slow or odd.
Yes, I periodically review and remove unknown devices.

recommendation.title: Monitor Connected Devices
recommendation.description: Unknown devices on your network can indicate neighbours or attackers using your Wi‑Fi.

text: Do you change your Wi-Fi password after guests or contractors have used it for a long period?

options.text: No, I keep the same Wi-Fi password for years.
Sometimes I change it, but not consistently.
Yes, I periodically rotate the Wi‑Fi password or use a guest network.

recommendation.title: Rotate Wi-Fi Credentials Periodically
recommendation.description: Changing Wi‑Fi passwords reduces long‑term access from old devices and people.

text: How do you secure network equipment physically (router, switches) at home or office?

options.text: They are easily accessible to anyone.
They are somewhat hidden but not locked.
They are placed in locations with limited access and clear labelling.

recommendation.title: Protect Network Hardware Physically
recommendation.description: Physical access to network devices can allow resets or tampering. Place them out of easy reach.

text: Do you avoid using public Wi-Fi for work-related confidential documents?

options.text: No, I often work on confidential items over public Wi-Fi.
I try to avoid it but sometimes must.
I use VPN or mobile hotspots for confidential work instead of open Wi-Fi.

recommendation.title: Protect Work Data on the Go
recommendation.description: Use secure channels like VPN or personal hotspots when handling confidential documents.

text: Do you hide your Wi-Fi network name (SSID) to reduce casual discovery?

options.text: No, SSID is fully visible.
I have considered it but not changed it.
Yes, or I use a non-identifying SSID that reveals nothing personal.

recommendation.title: Avoid Personal Information in SSID
recommendation.description: Even if not hidden, your Wi‑Fi name should not reveal your name or address.

text: Do you verify the legitimacy of public Wi-Fi hotspots before connecting (e.g., at hotels or cafes)?

options.text: No, I just connect to the strongest or most obvious network.
Sometimes I ask staff or check signage.
I always confirm the exact network name with staff and avoid lookalike networks.

recommendation.title: Confirm Public Wi-Fi Names
recommendation.description: Rogue hotspots mimic real names. Confirm details with staff before connecting.

text: Where do you primarily install apps from on your phone and computer?

options.text: From any website or store I find online.
Mostly official stores, but I sometimes sideload.
Only official app stores or trusted repositories.

recommendation.title: Stick to Official App Sources
recommendation.description: Untrusted sources can bundle malware with apps. Use official stores whenever possible.

text: How often do you review app permissions (camera, microphone, location, contacts)?

options.text: Never; I accept whatever is requested.
Occasionally, when something seems invasive.
Regularly, revoking permissions that are not necessary.

recommendation.title: Audit App Permissions Regularly
recommendation.description: Apps often request more access than needed. Remove permissions they don't truly require.

text: Do you grant 'always-on' location access to apps that do not obviously need it?

options.text: Yes, I usually allow it when asked.
Sometimes, if I think it might be useful.
No, I prefer 'while in use' or deny location when not needed.

recommendation.title: Restrict Background Location Access
recommendation.description: Limit continuous location tracking to maps and a few essential apps only.

text: How do you handle requests for camera and microphone access?

options.text: I usually tap 'Allow' without thinking.
I sometimes deny access if it feels unnecessary.
I allow access only when essential and revoke it afterward if possible.

recommendation.title: Be Selective With Camera/Mic Access
recommendation.description: Unnecessary access can allow apps to listen or watch without your knowledge.

text: Do you review which apps have access to your contacts, calendar, and files?

options.text: No, I haven't checked this.
I have looked once or twice.
I periodically review and revoke access for non-essential apps.

recommendation.title: Limit Access to Personal Data
recommendation.description: Contacts and calendar data can be used for spam or targeted phishing. Only trusted apps should see them.

text: How do you treat 'Sign in with Google/Apple/Facebook' permissions for third-party apps?

options.text: I grant all requested permissions to sign in quickly.
I sometimes customize permissions if possible.
I minimize requested scopes and periodically revoke access for unused apps.

recommendation.title: Control Third-Party Access via Single Sign-On
recommendation.description: Check what data an app can access via your SSO provider and revoke it if not needed.

text: Do you keep track of which apps or services have 'admin' or elevated privileges on your devices?

options.text: No, I do not track admin-level permissions.
I have a rough idea but haven't reviewed recently.
I actively review and restrict admin privileges to the minimum necessary.

recommendation.title: Restrict Admin Privileges
recommendation.description: Only a few trusted tools should have admin rights. Extra privileges increase potential damage.

text: How do you handle permissions for browser extensions and plugins?

options.text: I install many extensions and accept all permissions.
I use some extensions and skim their permissions.
I install few, well-reviewed extensions and review what they can access.

recommendation.title: Limit Powerful Browser Extensions
recommendation.description: Extensions can read everything in your browser. Keep them few and trustworthy.

text: Do you uninstall or revoke access for apps you no longer use?

options.text: No, unused apps remain installed indefinitely.
Sometimes I clean them up when storage is low.
I regularly uninstall unused apps to reduce attack surface.

recommendation.title: Remove Unused Apps
recommendation.description: Every extra app is another potential vulnerability. Delete what you do not use.

text: How do you approach app reviews and ratings before installing?

options.text: I rarely check reviews or publisher details.
I glance at ratings but not detailed reviews.
I check ratings, reviews, and the developer's reputation.

recommendation.title: Check Developer Reputation
recommendation.description: Reviews and publisher info help you avoid malicious or low‑quality apps.

text: Do you allow apps to run in the background with broad access when not in use?

options.text: Yes, most apps run freely in the background.
I sometimes limit background activity for battery reasons.
I actively limit background access except where clearly needed.

recommendation.title: Constrain Background App Activity
recommendation.description: Limiting background access reduces data collection and potential misuse.

text: Do you use app permission 'quick toggles' (e.g., temporary allow) when possible?

options.text: No, I either permanently allow or deny.
Sometimes I use them when prompted.
I prefer temporary permissions for one-time tasks.

recommendation.title: Use Temporary Permissions
recommendation.description: Granting one‑time access limits long‑term exposure if an app misbehaves later.

text: Do you regularly sign out of apps and web sessions on shared or public devices?

options.text: No, I rely on the app or browser to time out.
I sometimes sign out manually.
I always log out and clear data after using shared devices.

recommendation.title: Log Out on Shared Devices
recommendation.description: Open sessions can be hijacked. Logging out reduces this risk significantly.

text: Do you review security settings in major cloud accounts (Google, Microsoft, Apple) that manage app access?

options.text: No, I have never reviewed them.
I have looked once or twice.
I regularly review connected apps, devices, and sessions.

recommendation.title: Audit Cloud Account Security Dashboards
recommendation.description: Major providers show which apps and devices access your data. Clean up anything unnecessary.

text: Do you understand what data is collected by analytics and tracking permissions you grant to apps?

options.text: No, I usually ignore these notices.
I skim privacy notices but do not change behaviour.
I review privacy options and opt out of non-essential tracking where possible.

recommendation.title: Manage Analytics and Tracking
recommendation.description: Reduce unnecessary data collection by turning off optional tracking in app settings.

text: How do you handle permission requests from browser-based web apps?

options.text: I accept pop-ups for notifications, camera, or location without much thought.
I sometimes deny permissions that seem excessive.
I carefully review each request and only grant what is necessary.

recommendation.title: Apply the Same Discipline to Web App Permissions
recommendation.description: Browser permissions are as powerful as native app permissions. Approve only what you truly need.

text: Do you periodically export or review account activity logs from critical cloud services (storage, password manager, finance apps)?

options.text: No, I never look at these logs.
Occasionally, if I suspect an issue.
Yes, I periodically review logs for unusual access or operations.

recommendation.title: Review Critical App Activity Logs
recommendation.description: Logs help you detect unauthorized usage in storage, finance, or password tools early.
